adapter.native_library: dependency-light CUDA shared library built from cuda/src
adapter.torch_bridge: C++ dispatcher shim with checked public and unchecked IR hot-path operators
adapter.registration: vllm.general_plugins entry point and fused_add_rms_norm IR provider
adapter.compilation: passed torch.compile fullgraph and vLLM V1 compilation
adapter.cuda_graph: passed capture and replay in direct tests and the vLLM engine

raw_cuda.protocol: CUDA events, stable zero in-place fixture, 100 warmups, 15 samples, 2000 launches per sample

vllm_ir_microbenchmark.protocol: same vLLM IR maybe_inplace dispatch, 1 second BF16 GEMM clock warmup per provider, 100 operator warmups, 15 samples, 2000 launches per sample
vllm_ir_microbenchmark.correctness_reference: FP32 sum and reduction followed by BF16 output quantization
vllm_ir_microbenchmark.interpretation: Order-stable operator-level advantage: 10.6-11.4% through eager IR dispatch and about 3.5% under CUDA Graph replay.

workload.runner: normal vLLM Qwen2 V1 engine with compilation and CUDA Graphs

vllm_engine.token_note: Independent engine runs, including vllm_c versus vllm_c, diverged on this random model. Direct provider tests were bitwise identical, so cross-process token divergence is not attributed to Loom.
vllm_engine.interpretation: The adapter completes a real engine path, but the end-to-end runs are noisy and the isolated graph-replay saving is under 0.1 microsecond per call. No model-level speedup or regression is claimed.

claim: Loom is a working vLLM 0.24 fused Add+RMSNorm provider with H20 correctness, compilation, current-stream, and CUDA Graph evidence. It has an order-stable operator-level advantage over vllm_c, but no measurable end-to-end engine benefit has been established.
